RESEARCH PREPRINT FEBRUARY 2026 NUMERICAL LINEAR ALGEBRA

Fast Evaluation of
Truncated Neumann Series
by Low-Product Radix Kernels

Piyush Sao  ·  Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN, USA
1.53/log2k smallest asymptotic
coefficient achieved
Sk(A) = I + A + A² + ⋯ + A^(k−1) // GOAL — FEWER GEMMs // RADIX-9 · RADIX-15 · RADIX-24 // ~25% FASTER THAN BINARY // EXACT + CERTIFIED APPROXIMATE
0 ABSTRACT — THE POLYNOMIAL THAT WON'T DIE

Approximate inverses live and die by one cost: the matrix-matrix product.

Truncated Neumann series are a standard route to approximate inverses and polynomial preconditioners — but high-degree truncations are expensive because dense GEMMs dominate. Classical splitting methods already reach O(log k), so further progress hinges on finding better short product formulas for the geometric block I + B + ⋯ + Bm−1.

We contribute: (i) the first exact kernel beyond radix 5 — a radix-9 construction in 3 products; (ii) certified approximate kernels for radix 15 and 24 with bounded spillover; and (iii) a residual-based framework that uses approximate kernels repeatedly without losing control of the error. Net: the asymptotic matrix-product count drops by roughly one quarter versus repeated squaring.

3
Products for
radix-9 kernel
21%
Fewer GEMMs
vs binary split
4.5×10⁻¹⁴
Certified box radius
(radix-15)
mn
Prefix growth per
residual lift
1 THE SERIES

Why anyone cares about I + A + A² + ⋯

OPENING
For any A with spectral radius ρ(A) < 1, the truncated Neumann series converges to (I−A)⁻¹.
Sk(A)  =  Σj=0..k−1 Aj
         ⟶  (I − A)⁻¹
// finite k → practical approximate inverse
USES
Polynomial preconditioning · resolvent approximation · log-determinant estimation · massive MIMO detection.
Σ
Geometric
series
ρ<1
Convergence
regime
d×d
Dense
operands
2 THE BOTTLENECK

Every GEMM is expensive. There are too many of them.

CHALLENGE
For dense d ≳ 100, matrix-matrix products dominate. Naïve evaluation needs k−1 of them — prohibitive.
STATE
Binary splitting reaches 2 log₂ k; hybrid radix-{2,3} schemes edge down to ≈ 1.9 log₂ k. Asymptotic order is solved; the leading coefficient is the enemy.
Smn(A) = Sn(A) · Tm(An)
// Tm(B) = I + B + ⋯ + Bm−1 ← the "kernel"
cost/step  C(m) = μm + 2    coeff = C(m)/log₂m

Baseline coefficients

Binary
2.00
Ternary
1.89
Radix-5*
1.72

* Gustafsson et al. — the only prior low-product kernel. Everything above radix-5 was unknown.

3 THE FIRST BREAKTHROUGH — RADIX-9 CIRCUIT

An exact radix-9 kernel in just 3 matrix products.

TARGET
Degree-doubling bound gives μ9 ≥ 3. If reachable, the coefficient drops to 5/log₂9 ≈ 1.58 — a 21% saving over binary splitting.
IDEA
Build the third product around U = B² and V = B³ + 2B⁴. Seek one bilinear form P·Q whose convolution simultaneously fixes the high-degree terms B⁵ … B⁸; correct B²–B⁴ with additions only.

Sub-formulas

P  =  3⁄20 B  +  2U  +  V
Q  =  11⁄40 B  −  ⅛ U  +  ¼ V  +  ½ ·(−)
// linear combos of {B, U, V} — cost: 0 GEMMs
T9(B)  =  W  +  I  +  B  +  767⁄800 U  +  15⁄32 V
// rational coefficients — denominators divide 800
RESULT
First exact kernel beyond radix-5. Usable in rational or fixed-point arithmetic with zero spillover.

Circuit — Theorem 4.1  ·  3 GEMMs, 2 adders

3
GEMMs
2
Adders for P,Q
deg 8
Output
0
Spillover
4 GOING HIGHER — WITH CERTIFICATES

Radix 15 and 24: approximate, but provably tight.

CHALLENGE
Exact kernels beyond radix-9 may not exist (or are much harder to find). Approximate kernels have spillover into degrees ≥ m — breaking the clean telescoping identity.
ACTION
Bilinear chained-product ansatz → multi-start optimization → Krawczyk interval inclusion certifies a nearby exact root of the prefix system.
Fm(θ) = ([fθ]0−1, … , [fθ]m−1−1)
// prefix equations — certified exact at θ*
RESULT
Theorem 5.1 — certified radix-15 (4 products, K=4) and radix-24 (5 products, K=5) kernels exist in verified parameter boxes.

Certified error maps

E15(z) = 0.8148 z15
        − 0.2732 z16
        + 0.4584 z17
// interval radii < 2.1 × 10⁻¹³
E24(z) = 0.3552 z24 + 0.4310 z25
        + 0.1654 z26 − 0.1395 z27
        − 0.2417 z28 − 0.1016 z29
        + … + 0.0569 z33
// interval radii < 2.8 × 10⁻¹²
13
Local mfld dim
(radix-15)
17
Local mfld dim
(radix-24)
38
Subsystem
cond. est. ×10⁰
5 THE FRAMEWORK

Apply the kernel to the residual, not to powers of A.

CHALLENGE
Spillover breaks telescoping — Amn can no longer be extracted. Hensel lifting sidesteps this, but its coefficient (2.00) is no better than binary.
ACTION
Maintain an approximate inverse Yn ≈ M−1 and its residual Rn. Each step applies f to Rn directly.
M = I − A     Y0 = I, R0 = A
Yn+1 = Yn · f(Rn)
Rn+1 = I − M Yn+1
// cost per step: μm + 2 — same as exact
KEY LEMMA
Prefix growth: one lift upgrades an m-term exact prefix to an m²-term exact prefix. After n lifts: mⁿ terms exact, regardless of spillover.
Rn = E[n](A) = O(Amⁿ)
Yn = Σj=0..mⁿ−1 Aj + O(Amⁿ)

Residual iteration, drawn

6 STABILITY

When does repeated composition actually decay?

CHALLENGE
Prefix growth alone ≠ boundedness. We need a quantitative disk criterion on the error map E.
qρ := max|z|≤ρ |E(z)| / ρ
if  qρ ≤ 1   ⟹   disk {|z|≤ρ} forward invariant
// generalized Schwarz lemma
CERTIFIED
Crouzeix–Palencia lifts the scalar bound to the numerical range with constant CCr ≤ 1 + √2.

Certified radii

RADIX-15
ρq = 0.971
surrogate essentially sharp
RADIX-24
ρq = 0.984
direct circle test wins

Scalar stability curve — radix-24

7 RESOLUTION — GEMMs, COUNTED

The numbers, at k = 10¹⁵

23%
Products saved at k = 10¹⁵ vs binary splitting — 75 vs 98 GEMMs. The hybrid radix-24+ schedule wins at every scale.
METHODCOEF.10³10⁶10⁹10¹²10¹⁵
Binary2.001838587898
Quinary1.721834507086
Radix-91.581833486378
Radix-151.541634466476
Radix-241.531933476175
Radix-24+ best1429445975

Hybrid 24+ uses radix-24 for all but the final step, where a smaller radix may be cheaper. e.g. k ≥ 10³: (24, 24, 2) reaches 1152 in 14 products.

Cite as
Sao, P. Fast Evaluation of Truncated Neumann Series by Low-Product Radix Kernels. Research Preprint, Feb 2026. Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

  title = {Fast Evaluation of Truncated Neumann Series
        by Low-Product Radix Kernels},

  year = {2026}
}

Coefficient, visualised

Binary
2.00
Ternary
1.89
Quinary
1.72
Radix-9
1.58
Radix-15
1.54
Radix-24
1.53

Normal vs. nonnormal residual decay

8 TAKEAWAYS

One-quarter fewer GEMMs. Rigorous all the way down.

✓ RADIX-9
First exact kernel beyond radix-5 — rational coefficients, 3 products, coefficient 1.58.
✓ RADIX-15/24
Certified approximate kernels with interval-verified spillover and local manifold structure.
✓ FRAMEWORK
Residual iteration absorbs spillover while preserving cost (μm + 2)/log₂m. Hensel & exact splitting appear as special cases.
✓ STABILITY
Matrix-level bounds via Crouzeix–Palencia; certified radii 0.971 (r-15) and 0.984 (r-24).

Open

Exact rational kernels for m ≥ 15 — existence unknown. Global nonexistence is not proved; our Jacobian analysis is local.

Acknowledgements

This work was supported by the Laboratory Directed Research and Development (LDRD) program at Oak Ridge National Laboratory — program manager Sudip Seal. ORNL is managed by UT-Battelle, LLC for the U.S. Department of Energy under Contract DE-AC05-00OR22725.

R REFERENCES & RELATED WORK

Prior art this paper extends and rests on.

Neumann-series splitting

  1. Westreich, D. (1989). A fast algorithm for the matrix exponential and truncated Neumann series. Comput. Math. Appl. — binary splitting, 3 log₂k products.
  2. Lei, M. & Nakamura, Y. (2002). Improved splitting reusing intermediate computations — 2 log₂k − 1 products.
  3. Dimitrov, V. & Cooklev, T. (1995). Hybrid radix-{2,3} schemes for arbitrary k, coefficient ≈ 1.9.
  4. Gustafsson, O. et al. (2020). Quinary (radix-5) low-product kernel — the only prior μm < m construction.

Matrix-polynomial evaluation

  1. Paterson, M. S. & Stockmeyer, L. J. (1973). On the number of nonscalar multiplications necessary to evaluate polynomials — O(√n) baseline.
  2. Fasi, M. & Higham, N. J. (2019). Optimality within the Paterson–Stockmeyer scheme.
  3. Sastre, J. & Ibáñez, J. (2021). Composition methods reaching degree 15 in 4 products for exponential-family polynomials.
  4. Matsumoto, Takagi & Takagi. Higher radices can improve the leading coefficient (no explicit circuits).

Stability & applications

  1. Crouzeix, M. & Palencia, C. (2017). The numerical range is a (1+√2)-spectral set.
  2. Polynomial preconditioning — Saad, Y. Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems.
  3. Resolvent approximation — Trefethen & Embree, Spectra and Pseudospectra.
  4. Log-det estimation — Han, Malioutov, Shin (2015).
  5. Massive MIMO detection — Yin, Vosoughi, Studer (2013).
